title: The Butterfly Effect
definition: The sensitive dependence on initial conditions in chaotic systems: small changes in initial state can lead to vastly different outcomes. A butterfly flapping its wings could theoretically cause a tornado weeks later.

proofs.description: Edward Lorenz's weather model (1963) showed extreme sensitivity to rounding errors
proofs.reference: Lorenz, E.N. (1963). Deterministic Nonperiodic Flow. Journal of the Atmospheric Sciences, 20(2), 130-141.

proofs.description: Reproducible in many dynamical systems (double pendulum, three-body problem)
proofs.reference: Strogatz, S.H. (1994). Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos.

difficulty_levels.beginner: Small changes can have big effects. A tiny difference now can lead to completely different outcomes later.
difficulty_levels.intermediate: In chaotic systems, small differences in initial conditions amplify exponentially, making long-term prediction impossible even though systems are deterministic. This is the butterfly effect.
difficulty_levels.expert: The butterfly effect is characterized by positive Lyapunov exponents, indicating exponential divergence of nearby trajectories. It implies practical unpredictability beyond the Lyapunov horizon despite deterministic equations. Found in weather, population dynamics, fluid turbulence, and neural networks.

extra_fields.historical_context: Term coined by Edward Lorenz (1972) in paper 'Predictability: Does the Flap of a Butterfly's Wings in Brazil Set Off a Tornado in Texas?'
extra_fields.limitations: Effect size is exponential but requires long time horizons. Not all systems are chaotic; linear and stable systems don't show butterfly effect.
